gold.rationale: The article’s core event facts align with the source material, including the date, duration, crew roles, successful completion, and debris-protection installation. However, it mildly overstates the strategic significance and urgency of the EVA, and the edited visuals intensify the sense of peril and importance beyond what the underlying evidence alone supports.

visual_indicators.scene_description: An astronaut in a white spacesuit with blue stripes is floating in space, attached to a robotic arm mechanism. The astronaut is manipulating a white panel or equipment. The Earth's atmosphere and clouds are visible in the background against the blackness of space.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows an astronaut performing an EVA, consistent with the text's description of a spacewalk.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A close-up view of a gloved hand in a white spacesuit reaching toward complex mechanical hardware. An orange safety tether or cable is visible in the foreground. A grid-like structure, possibly a solar panel, is in the background.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows detailed work on station hardware, consistent with the text's description of installing equipment.

visual_indicators.scene_description: Two astronauts in white spacesuits are inside a spacecraft module. An open circular hatchway leads to the blackness of space. A blue and red flag patch is visible on the shoulder of the astronaut in the foreground. A blue logo is on the back of the suit. Orange safety tethers are attached to the astronauts.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows astronauts inside a module near a hatch, consistent with the text's description of crew members inside the station.

visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows a space station, consistent with the text's description of Tiangong.

visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows an astronaut in space, consistent with the text's description of an EVA.

visual_indicators.scene_description: Two astronauts in white spacesuits are inside a spacecraft module. An open circular hatchway leads to space. A blue logo is on the back of the astronaut's suit. A watermark with Chinese text '@中国军号' is in the bottom right corner.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows astronauts inside a module, consistent with the text's description of crew members inside the station. The watermark indicates a Chinese source.

retrieved_evidence.text: Descriptions of the Shenzhou-21 mission’s second extravehicular activity emphasize the hostile nature of the environment surrounding Tiangong, where astronauts worked tethered to the station structure against the blackness of space. According to officials, the crew operated in bulky Feitian suits while attaching new hardware associated with station maintenance and debris shielding, relying on safety lines, footholds, and robotic assistance to remain secure. The accounts underline how each movement must be carefully choreographed, as the combination of vacuum, extreme temperatures, and microgravity leaves little margin for error during such operations.

Close-up accounts from the Shenzhou-21 mission emphasize the intricate manual work required during the second spacewalk as astronauts in pressurized suits manipulated tools and fittings on the outer hull of Tiangong. In recent reports, Chinese outlets have highlighted gloved hands fastening brackets, connectors, and debris-protection panels in precisely defined locations along the station’s exterior. According to officials, these tasks formed part of broader maintenance and upgrade operations intended to safeguard the orbiting complex during its continued operation in low Earth orbit.

Chinese space officials stated that astronauts Zhang Lu and Wu Fei carried out the recent Shenzhou-21 spacewalk, while their crewmate Zhang Hongzhang remained inside the Tiangong space station to provide operational support. According to state media coverage published in recent days, Zhang Lu and Wu Fei exited the station via an airlock after a series of safety checks, while Zhang Hongzhang monitored systems and communications from within the orbital complex. Mission managers said the division of roles was arranged in advance as part of standard procedures.

China’s human spaceflight agency said in a statement that the second spacewalk of the Shenzhou-21 mission was completed at 19:35 Beijing time on 16 March 2026, according to reports carried by state media in recent days. The operation took place outside the country’s Tiangong space station, which is in low Earth orbit, and was described as part of the crew’s routine work schedule for the current expedition. Officials added that detailed technical data from the activity would be further assessed by ground teams in the coming weeks.

Inside the Tiangong space station, coordination for the Shenzhou-21 crew’s second spacewalk focused heavily on procedures around the Wentian lab module hatch. In recent reports, mission specialists have described how the intravehicular astronaut monitored suit telemetry, operated controls, and supported the two spacewalkers as they cycled through depressurization and reentry after roughly seven hours outside. The hatch area served as a compact staging zone where checklists, communication links, and emergency provisions were organized to ensure safe completion of the extravehicular activity.

China’s human spaceflight agency described the latest Shenzhou-21 spacewalk as a complete success, according to statements carried by state media in recent days. Officials said that all planned tasks outside the Tiangong space station were carried out largely as scheduled and that the crew’s performance met mission expectations. While some technical assessments are still being compiled by engineers on the ground, the agency emphasized that the outcome represents another step in the routine operation of the country’s space station program.

Accounts of China’s human spaceflight program emphasize that the Shenzhou-21 crew’s second spacewalk is one in a series of planned extravehicular activities aimed at consolidating Tiangong’s long-term capabilities. According to officials, each EVA builds on procedures tested during earlier missions, refining toolkits, timelines, and emergency protocols. The March 2026 outing, which lasted about seven hours, has been cited by analysts as evidence that Chinese astronauts are developing routine proficiency in tasks ranging from hardware installation to external inspections of critical station systems.

According to information released this week by China’s crewed spaceflight authorities, the extravehicular activity conducted during the latest Shenzhou-21 mission lasted for about seven hours. Early reports indicate that the operation followed a pre-planned sequence of tasks around the Tiangong space station’s modules, with timelines and procedures coordinated from control centers on the ground. Officials said the overall duration remained within the expected range for such activities, though a full analysis of performance is still under way.

The crew of the Shenzhou-21 mission coordinated closely with both the Tiangong space station’s robotic arm and ground-based support teams during their latest spacewalk, according to officials from China’s spaceflight program. Mission controllers at Beijing’s space control center were reported to have provided continuous guidance, including adjustments to the robotic arm to assist the astronauts’ movements and equipment handling outside the station. Engineers said such joint operations between onboard systems and ground personnel are now a regular feature of China’s extravehicular activities.

In March 2026, China’s Shenzhou-21 mission carried out its second spacewalk outside the Tiangong space station, with Chinese astronauts working along the station’s exterior while Earth filled the distant backdrop. According to official releases, one crew member moved along a robotic arm and external handrails to reach work sites where new debris-protection hardware was installed. The extravehicular activity lasted roughly seven hours before the spacewalkers returned safely to the Wentian lab module, underscoring China’s growing experience with complex orbital engineering tasks.

Chinese space authorities reported that the two astronauts who conducted the latest Shenzhou-21 spacewalk returned safely to the Wentian laboratory module of the Tiangong space station at the end of their operation. According to early accounts from mission control, the crew re-entered the airlock following standard procedures, including pressure checks and equipment stowage, before removing their spacesuits. Officials said the safe completion of the ingress phase marked the formal conclusion of the extravehicular activity for this shift.

Recent summaries of China’s Tiangong program point out that the Shenzhou-21 crew’s successful completion of their second extravehicular activity contributes to a growing body of operational experience in human spaceflight. Officials have indicated that lessons learned from the roughly seven-hour spacewalk, including tool handling, crew coordination, and use of the station’s robotic systems, will inform future missions and possible expansions of the orbital outpost. As Tiangong continues to serve as a platform for both scientific research and technology demonstration, such EVAs play a central role in validating China’s long-term plans for sustained presence in space.

One of the primary objectives of the most recent Shenzhou-21 spacewalk was the installation of equipment intended to enhance protection against space debris, according to statements released by China’s human spaceflight authorities. State media reports said the astronauts worked on the exterior of the Tiangong space station to mount or adjust protective components in areas identified by engineers as relatively exposed. Officials noted that these measures are part of ongoing efforts to reduce the risk posed by small orbiting fragments, with further technical details yet to be fully disclosed.

Recent Chinese media coverage of the Shenzhou-21 mission has noted that astronaut Zhang Lu has already taken part in multiple spacewalks during his career, suggesting a growing level of experience with extravehicular operations. Officials have not provided a precise tally for all past activities in which he has participated, but described him as an astronaut with established familiarity in working outside the spacecraft. Commentators in China’s space sector say such repeated assignments may help build operational expertise for future missions.

China’s Tiangong space station, where the Shenzhou-21 crew is currently based, is described by officials as a permanently inhabited orbital outpost in low Earth orbit, designed to support long-duration scientific experiments and technology demonstrations. According to background information from the country’s human spaceflight agency, the complex consists of several interconnected modules, including core and laboratory sections that provide living quarters and research facilities. For readers unfamiliar with the program, Tiangong is intended to serve as the centerpiece of China’s independent space station effort for at least a decade, hosting rotating crews of three astronauts at a time.

China’s Tiangong space station, as detailed in official diagrams and public briefings, consists of a core module flanked by laboratory modules including Wentian, which hosted the return point for the Shenzhou-21 crew’s second spacewalk. In recent technical explanations aimed at international audiences, Chinese space authorities have outlined how Wentian provides additional living space, experiment racks, and an airlock that supports extravehicular operations. Understanding this modular architecture helps clarify where external debris-protection devices were installed and how the station is designed to expand over time in low Earth orbit.

Technical briefings related to the Shenzhou-21 mission have highlighted the design and purpose of debris-protection equipment now mounted on Tiangong’s exterior. In recent reports, engineers from the China Manned Space Agency described multilayered panels and shields intended to deflect or absorb impacts from micrometeoroids and orbital fragments that could threaten pressurized modules like Wentian. These additions form part of a wider risk-mitigation strategy, complementing orbital debris tracking and collision-avoidance maneuvers that help keep the station and its crew safe over multiyear operations.

Background materials from China’s space authorities stress the role of Wentian as both a laboratory and operational hub supporting spacewalks like the Shenzhou-21 crew’s second excursion. In recent technical overviews, Wentian is described as housing life-support subsystems, scientific payloads, and control consoles that tie into Tiangong’s power and guidance networks. The module’s external surfaces provide mounting points for experiments and debris shields, while its airlock enables astronauts to access the station’s outer structure for maintenance, thereby linking internal research activities with ongoing infrastructure upgrades.

【赞！#中国人在太空出了一趟门#】#中国空间站又升级了##神二十圆满完成第2次出舱活动#北京时间2025年6月26日21时29分，经过约6.5小时的出舱活动，神舟二十号乘组航天员陈冬、陈中瑞、王杰密切协同，在空间站机械臂和地面科研人员的配合支持下，圆满完成既定任务，出舱航天员陈冬、陈中瑞已安全返回问 ​​​

【#神21乘组圆满完成第二次出舱活动#】3月16日19时35分，经过7小时的出舱活动，神舟二十一号乘组航天员张陆、武飞、张洪章密切协同，在空间站机械臂和地面科研人员的配合支持下，圆满完成空间碎片防护装置安装等任务。出舱航天员张陆、武飞已安全返回问天实验舱，出舱活动取得圆满成功。

International commentary on the Shenzhou-21 mission often situates the second spacewalk within the broader context of global orbital operations and the challenge posed by space debris. Analysts in recent reports have noted that by installing additional protective hardware on Tiangong, China is aligning with practices adopted on other long-duration stations to mitigate impact risks. The activity demonstrates not only engineering capability but also an acknowledgment of the increasingly crowded low Earth orbit environment, where even small fragments can damage modules such as Wentian over time.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately reports the specific details of the Shenzhou-21 EVA (crew members, duration, date, tasks) as corroborated by multiple evidence sources, with no fabricated facts, distortions, or context errors.
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is a faithful and detailed account of the Shenzhou-21 second EVA, aligning perfectly with official reports and social media evidence regarding crew roles, timing, and mission objectives.

judge.level3_rationale: The post accurately reports the Shenzhou-21 crew's second spacewalk, including the date, duration, crew roles, and tasks, all of which are fully supported by official evidence and consistent with the accompanying images.
judge.reasoning_trace: All key subclaims were verified against multiple news snippets and social posts from official sources. The images were checked for consistency with the event and found to be appropriate representations without synthetic or misleading edits.
judge.core_claim: The Shenzhou-21 crew successfully completed their second spacewalk on March 16, 2026, installing debris protection equipment on the Tiangong space station.
key_subclaims: The Shenzhou-21 crew's second EVA ended at 19:35 Beijing time on March 16, 2026.
The spacewalk lasted about seven hours.
Astronauts Zhang Lu and Wu Fei performed the EVA while Zhang Hongzhang supported from inside.
The crew installed space-debris protection equipment.
The astronauts returned safely to the Wentian laboratory module.
Chinese authorities described the mission as a complete success.

subclaim_findings.note: Evidence explicitly confirms the date and time.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The spacewalk lasted about seven hours.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the duration was about seven hours.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Astronauts Zhang Lu and Wu Fei performed the EVA while Zhang Hongzhang supported from inside.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the crew roles and names.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The crew installed space-debris protection equipment.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the installation of debris protection equipment.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The astronauts returned safely to the Wentian laboratory module.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the return to the Wentian module.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Chinese authorities described the mission as a complete success.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the mission was described as a complete success.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post accurately reports the specific details of the Shenzhou-21 EVA, including dates, times, crew members, and tasks, all of which are corroborated by the retrieved evidence. The images provided are consistent with the text and the evidence descriptions, showing appropriate scenes of the spacewalk and station interior. There are no fabrications, distortions, or misleading contexts detected.
